txpartsupport: [i]NOTE: (β) This language is only partially supported. Therefore, some sections are in English. Support is welcome (Github). - On the last page, you will find an [ref=autotr][u]‘Auto-Translate’[/u][/ref] button[/i]
txnosupport: [i]NOTE: Your language is not (yet) supported, so English is shown. An [ref=autotr][u]‘Auto-Translate’[/u][/ref] button is available on the last page[/i]
txautotranslate: [i]NOTE: (α) This language is not (yet) supported. This is a machine translation. (Machine-translated languages may be temporarily or permanently unavailable.)[/i]
txanleitung: [DF]{LANGSUPPORT}1. Enable the checkbox {txgpsauto} to automatically update your location during use. ({txgpsbutton} determines your current position once and stores it.) Nothing more is required for now. – For higher accuracy see {quotes|0-}4. {txshowagni-}{quotes|1-}.[~LF][~LF]Alternatively, you may enter a place name or an address into the text field (if supported by your device) or coordinates (e.g. 50.94, 6.96). A 3rd and 4th entry are optional: approximate altitude &bl;m], approximate temperature at twilight &bl;°C]. Then enable the checkbox {txwelcomescr}. – When basic data are changed, {txsunclock-} performs a daily calculation which may take between 1[NDASH]30 seconds depending on the device.[~LF][~LF]2.{txuhrsinn} defines whether the clock runs clockwise (south) or counter-clockwise: when changing the direction of rotation, the part of the solar circle below the horizon is positioned in front of you (details see next page). {txshowclock} displays a digital clock at the upper right of the dial.
txmore: [DF]3. {txnachtmodus} {txlangarr|auto-} switches at sunset, -6° at the end of twilight, +6° before. {txfarben} selects different dials. {txedgescreen} increases the distance to the screen edge.[~LF][~LF]4. {txfullscr} prevents the screen from turning off and switches to full screen. {txweathapi} displays a forecast on the clock (see below, internet required). {txagnihotra} shows markers for the sun hand as well as the time of the next zero crossing of the sun & countdown. Six minutes before Agnihotra, the display is enlarged and seconds are shown ({s_fr-} = GPS current). If your settings are not applied at any point, tap the [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]⟳[/font] button at the bottom right of the clock face.[~LF][~LF]You can zoom into the clock face with two fingers (or with a triple tap). If you want to move the zoomed view, do it slowly. Fast movements swipe to the next page. Use the [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]⬌[/font] button to activate {txtimecontrol} (see next page).
txlegende: [DF][b]–– Legend:[/b][~LF][~LF]Weather: from bottom to top, current temperature, daily precipitation data ({s_sn-} = snow) and hours of sunshine; for symbols, see the next page[DF][DF][DF]
txlocdata: [DF]Calculations for your location correspond with the astronomical almanac to approx. 0.0005 arcseconds³:[DF]
txobjdata: {weather_data}– Sun¹ –[~LF]Time: {sun_clocktm}[~LF]Temporal hour: {sun_temptm}[~LF]Daylight: {sun_daylight}{sun_lighttime}{sun_agnicountdown}{sun_agnihotra}[~LF]Solstice: {sun_soli}[~LF]Equinox: {sun_equi}[~LF][~LF]– Moon² –[~LF]Lunar night: {moon_gordon}[~LF]Illumination: {moon_illum}[~LF]Size: {moon_sizep} (85-100)[~LF]New Moon: {moon_new}[~LF]Full Moon: {moon_full}[~LF][~LF]– Brightness –[~LF]Venus: {venus_magp} (83-100)[~LF]Mars: {mars_magp} (52-100)[~LF]Jupiter: {jupiter_magp} (87-100)[~LF]Saturn: {saturn_magp} (77-100)[~LF]Mercury: {merkur_magp} (0-100)[~LF][~LF]¹ Time: GPS = sync | Temporal hour: local unequal hour (0.0[NDASH]11.9), {s_td} day / {s_tn} night. {s_tv} night watch (0{s_tv-}00[NDASH]3{s_tv-}59) | {lighttime-}: see below | Daylight & Agnihotra: sunrise[NDASH]sunset today {sb0}sunrise tomorrow{sb1}, {s_fr-} = current GPS | ² Full/New Moon: moment (in days/days ago) | Lunar night: see below | ³ Moon ≈ 2000 arcsec | ⁴ Weather: Daily progression ([font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☀[/font] sun, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]◴[/font] progress, ₁₂₃ = current 3h block, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☂[/font] rain clickable = radar, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]✈[/font] gusts). Now = next 3 hours, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]◵[/font] = older data. Display limited to 48 hours for reliability | Below daily values: min...max...{sb0}night min{sb1} ({s_sn-} = snow)[DF][DF]
txlegende0: [b]–– Explanations:[/b][~LF][~LF]Lunar night works like our calendar date, but with nights: {s_mo-}x·y (p/z) where x = lunar {quotes|0-}month{quotes|1-} of the year, y = night of the lunar month. {s_mo-}1 is the first full moon of the light year (≈ Chinese New Year). As lunar months have different lengths, z indicates the total nights of the lunar month, p the full-moon night. The night ends at sunrise.[~LF][~LF]The light-year season divides the year into eight phases of increasing and decreasing light: 1. {solcal_lph|0-} ({solcal_dat0}), 2a. {solcal_lph|1-} ({solcal_dat1}), 2b. {solcal_lph|2-} ({solcal_dat2}), 2c. {solcal_lph|3-} ({solcal_dat3}), 3. {solcal_lph|4-} ({solcal_dat4}), 4a. {solcal_lph|5-} ({solcal_dat5}), 4b. {solcal_lph|6-} ({solcal_dat6}) and 4c. {solcal_lph|7-} ({solcal_dat7}). Short form: {s_li-}x·y (x = phase, y =[SP]% progress). The data change slightly each year.[DF][DF][DF]
txanleitung2: [DF]{txgordonssunclock} is essentially a single-hand clock with a dial that changes daily, oriented to the rhythms of nature and the celestial bodies. It shows the position of the Sun and the starry sky. A new kind of time display that is not artificial like our conventional clock (with railway time and daylight saving time), but moves in harmony with the seasons.[~LF][~LF][i]NOTE: You can swipe back and forth between this guide and the dial.[/i][~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Your view:[/b][~LF][~LF]In the centre of the screen you see a horizontal line. This is the horizon (the Earth's surface). The pointed triangle in the middle is a spruce tree that you see from a distance (or a church). You are not looking down at it from above, but standing in front of it.[~LF][~LF]If you have not changed anything in the settings ({quotes|0-}{txuhrsinn-}: {txlangarr|auto-}{quotes|1-}), you are usually facing south in the Northern Hemisphere (north in the Southern Hemisphere). Objects on the left of the screen are in the east, on the right in the west (reversed in the Southern Hemisphere). The respective cardinal directions are shown at the edge of the horizon.[~LF][~LF]The lighter band below the horizon indicates civil twilight. The large dark grey area symbolises the night (including nautical twilight). Objects located in this area are not visible.[~LF][~LF]At the top right you see the local time (optional), the altitude of the Sun in degrees in the sky (e.g. 27.6°), as well as your position as a numerical code (e.g. {quotes|0-}cri-hs{quotes|1-}) or a place name if you have entered one. Below that the light season and the unequal temporal hour display.
txanleitung2b: [DF][DF][b]–– Sun:[/b][~LF][~LF]The [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☉[/font] sun (at the end of the clock’s single hand) moves from left to right over the course of the day (reversed in the southern hemisphere). The further left the sun is on the screen, the more it is in the east; the further right, the more in the west (reversed in the southern hemisphere). The higher the sun appears on the screen, the higher it is in the sky.[~LF][~LF]The sun follows the drawn circular path along which it moves within a day. At the edge of this circle, hours are indicated (e.g. {quotes|0-}12{quotes|1-} o’clock), so the circle also functions as a clock face.[~LF][~LF]On the clock face, for example, one can see how during daylight saving time the numbers shift, while the circular path remains the same. Or: how legal time at most places differs from local solar time, as 12 o’clock is not exactly at the top of the dial. Temporal hours do it differently: at the 12th hour (12{s_td-}), it is always sunset.[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Moon:[/b][~LF][~LF]The [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]●[/font] moon also has a circular path, shown in light grey. Since the moon takes more than 24 hours to complete one orbit, 1.5 hours from the previous day are appended in extra light grey. If one {quotes|0-}lives with the clock{quotes|1-}, one can nicely observe how the moon spirals higher and lower over the course of 27 days. One can also observe how at full moon it stands opposite the sun on the circle (distant); at new moon, however, it stands at the same position on the circle (close to the sun). It is rarely exactly the same position because the moon’s orbit is inclined relative to the sun’s path. It is exactly the same position only during a solar eclipse.
txanleitung2c: [DF]New moon is displayed for 48 hours because the crescent is usually not visible to the naked eye during this period. Full moon is shown for 24 hours. The recalculation of the sun and moon paths takes place at UTC 00:00.[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Planets:[/b][~LF][~LF]The five planets visible to the naked eye are shown, depending on the design either with photos as seen through binoculars or with their astronomical symbols. When you look at the real night sky, you can distinguish planets from stars by knowing that planets do not twinkle but shine steadily. They appear larger in the sky than the distant stars and are therefore less affected by atmospheric turbulence.[~LF][~LF][font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♀[/font] Venus (pearl-coloured), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♂[/font] Mars (reddish), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♃[/font] Jupiter (striped), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♄[/font] Saturn (with rings), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☿[/font] Mercury (grey)[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Stars:[/b][~LF][~LF]In addition, the ten brightest stars are shown, represented by a letter in a square. While the planets move relatively orderly, mostly more or less near the sun’s path, the path of the stars is independent of the sun — after all, they are suns themselves. In the star symbols, two sides of the square are always drawn more strongly than the other two. This indicates the corner or quadrant of their path in which they are currently located. If the highlighted corner is bottom right, the star is (more or less) at the “end” of its path downwards and to the right. In its further movement it will mainly move upwards and to the left (often also outside the screen). If the upper side is stronger, the star is in front of you; if the lower side is stronger, it is behind you (provided you have not changed the direction of rotation).
txmore2: [DF]&bl;s] Sirius, &bl;k] Canopus, &bl;f] Alpha Centauri, &bl;a] Arcturus, &bl;w] Vega, &bl;c] Capella, &bl;r] Rigel, &bl;p] Procyon, &bl;b] Betelgeuse, &bl;h] Achernar[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Meteors:[/b][~LF][~LF]Each year, Earth passes through dust trails of small celestial bodies. This produces meteors. During the five main meteor showers, the area is marked where meteors are most frequently visible (though they can appear anywhere). When visibility is good, the search area is shown in red, and many meteors per hour are possible. The best time is usually the second half of the night, as Earth then rotates into the dust stream. A red square with letters marks the point from which they appear to originate. As with the stars, two sides are drawn more prominently; they indicate the direction of the search area (top = in front of you, bottom = behind you, relative to your viewing direction). Data vary slightly each year:[~LF][~LF]&bl;P] Perseids ({range_P}), &bl;O] Orionids ({range_O}), &bl;L] Leonids ({range_L}), &bl;G] Geminids ({range_G}), &bl;Q] Quadrantids ({range_Q})[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– {txtimecontrol-}:[/b][~LF][~LF]If you want to move forwards or backwards in time, press the [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]⬌[/font] button. Then press and hold in the centre of the screen and slowly swipe left or right. The vertical position determines the step size: at the top years, at the bottom minutes. Upon release, the dial is recalculated (duration 1[NDASH]30s). Double-tap to return to now. Swipe at the very bottom or top to switch pages. If you want to go further back in time, e.g. to your birth sky, additional ephemeris data can be downloaded (years 1900[NDASH]2050, see next page).
txanleitung5: [DF][DF][b]–– Weather:[/b][~LF][~LF]At the bottom left, a local weather forecast can be displayed (details previous page below). It presents the character of the day in a composed manner using symbols: for example, {wx/rain} stands for actual rain (6[NDASH]18 o’clock), not occasional drizzle (analogously: {wx/rain2} heavy rain, {wx/snow} snow, {wx/uncertain} uncertain, {wx/dry} dry, {wx/sun} sunny, {wx/sun2} lots of sun, {wx/still} calm, {wx/wind} windy, {wx/storm} storm, {wx/storm2} gale-force). No symbol means unremarkable weather conditions. From bottom to top: current temperature, daily precipitation data ({s_sn-} = snow) and sunshine hours; weekday = tomorrow; grey = old data, {s_fr-} = current. The weather data are also used for atmospheric refraction and slightly influence the timing of sunrise and sunset.
txanleitung2d: [DF][DF][b]–– History:[/b][~LF][~LF]{txgordonssunclock} is the result of more than twenty years of engagement with the representation of time. The starting point was a mechanical organ clock with a 24 h dial and a month-dependent display of sunrise and sunset. From this emerged the concept of a {txsunclock-}, initially as a feasibility study and an ePaper wall clock; later, weather display and GPS were added. The idea of a location-flexible version culminated in this Android app.[~LF][~LF]The goal of the development is a representation of time that follows the course of the Sun and the natural daily and annual cycles – as a reconnection of humans with nature and with our inner, circadian time. May it provide insight and understanding.[DF][DF][DF]
txanleitung3: [DF]This software is a private project. Please use it as it is. In principle, no personal data is collected or transmitted. No internet connection is required for operation. Details on optional features (weather display, place name resolution), error reports, and update checks can be found in the privacy policy.[DF][DF]
txanleitung4: [DF]If you have found an error, I will gladly correct it. For this I need a description of where and when the error occurred (possibly with a screenshot). A screenshot of the log below taken as soon as possible after the error occurred is also helpful (mail contact: [CONTACTSUPPORT]).[~LF][~LF]Thanks to: Brandon Rhodes (Skyfield), Rolf, Greg Miller and all testers. Images: NASA/ SOHO, ESA/ Hubble, NASA/ JPL-Caltech (public domain). Weather data: Open-Meteo.com (CC BY 4.0), MET Norway (CC BY 4.0). Details: {quotes|0-}design/ COPYRIGHT.txt{quotes|1-}.[~LF][~LF]I provide {txsunclock} to you free of charge. Simply enjoy it. If you would like to support my work, you can find my website here. - For {txtimecontrol} you can download additional ephemeris data here (and delete them again: ~17 MB, see previous page).[DF][DF]
